text: This is a video for a combined robotic sigmoidectomy and robotic left hepatectomy by Drs. Chudzinski and Dr. Sukhandi at Florida Hospital Tampa in Tampa, Florida. This is a fifty-six-year-old male who had presented with a sigmoid colon mass approximately forty centimeters from the anal verge. It was biopsy positive for adenocarcinoma.
He underwent neoadjuvant chemotherapy with Folfax and Avastin and responded very appropriately. He was presented at multidisciplinary tumor board and was deemed to be an excellent surgical candidate.

The CT scan reveals the tumor located in the sigmoid colon. Following the placement of robotic ports, a formal laparoscopy is performed, identifying the mass and the distal tattoo marking its location.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealing device to take down peritoneal adhesions. Lighted ureteral stents are visible, enhanced with intraoperative fluorescent imaging techniques.

The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric vessels and confirms the ureter's location. A white load robotic stapler is used to transect the vessels, followed by the robotic vessel sealing device to take down the mesentery.

The surgeon continues dissection along the mesentery in both proximal and distal directions, ensuring that the mesentery is taken down until grossly negative margins are visible, away from the site of the tattoo.

The surgeon uses a green load robotic stapler to transect the distal sigmoid colon from the proximal rectum, thinning out the colonic mesentery. The specimen is extracorporealized, transected, and an EEA anvil is placed. The descending colon is then returned to the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon inserts an EEA stapler through the rectal stump to perform a circular stapled anastomosis. After completing the anastomosis, the stapler is removed, and the pelvis is filled with saline. The descending colon is clamped, and a rigid sigmoidoscope is reinserted into the rectum, confirming a negative leak test.
The robotic system is docked to begin the liver portion of the hepatectomy. An intraoperative ultrasound probe is used to identify the liver lesion, providing real-time imaging of the targeted area.

The surgeon uses monopolar instrumentation to take down the falciform ligament, followed by the left triangular and coronary ligaments while holding the left lobe of the liver. The gastrohepatic ligament is also opened, revealing the liver's anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to identify the plane of liver transection, marking out the resection margins with monopolar instrumentation.
The surgeon opens the resection plane using monopolar instrumentation, followed by the use of a vessel sealing device to further transect the tissue.

The surgeon maintains hemostasis using a vessel sealing device during the liver transection, which extends more than two centimeters deep. A laparoscopic vascular stapling device is then employed to complete the transection.

The surgeon divides the left hepatic vein intrahepatically, using monopolar instrumentation to achieve further hemostasis. Additional suturing is performed to ensure excellent hemostasis and prevent biliary leakage.
The resected liver tissue is placed in a retrieval bag for removal. Fibrin sealant is applied over the transection margin to ensure hemostasis and prevent leakage.

This is a fifty-six-year-old male who underwent a combined robotic sigmoidectomy and robotic left liver resection. He tolerated the procedure well. The final pathology was T-N-M of invasive moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. He was discharged on post-operative day three, tolerating a regular diet. His pain was well controlled and he had normal bowel function.
